title: Therapeutic Communication: Building the Nurse-Patient Relationship
subtitle: A patient newly diagnosed with a serious illness becomes tearful and withdrawn. The nurse uses therapeutic communication — open-ended questions, active listening, silence, and empathy — while avoiding nontherapeutic blocks like false reassurance and 'why' questions.

patient.name: Therapeutic Communication
patient.admitDate: A patient newly diagnosed with a serious illness becomes tearful and quiet. The nurse uses therapeutic techniques to build trust and explore feelings while avoiding communication blocks.

nursesNote: A patient newly diagnosed with a serious illness becomes tearful, quiet, and withdrawn. Therapeutic communication is patient-centered and goal-directed: the nurse sits down, gives full attention, and uses open-ended questions, active listening, silence, reflection, and empathy to help the patient express feelings. The nurse clarifies and validates understanding and provides honest information without false reassurance. The nurse avoids communication blocks — clichés, false reassurance, giving personal advice, judgmental 'why' questions, and changing the subject — and stays alert to cultural needs and privacy. When the patient voices hopelessness, the nurse calmly and directly assesses for suicidal thoughts, ensures safety, notifies the provider, and documents.
providerOrders: Use open-ended questions, active listening, and silence to explore feelings
Show empathy; reflect and validate the patient's concerns
Avoid false reassurance, advice, 'why' questions, and changing the subject
Provide a private, unhurried setting and respect cultural needs
Directly assess safety if the patient expresses hopelessness; notify provider and document

opts.text: Using open-ended questions to encourage the patient to elaborate
Offering silence to give the patient time to think and respond
Reflecting and restating to confirm what the patient means
Telling the patient "Don't worry, everything will be fine"
Active listening with attentive body language and eye contact
Asking "Why did you stop your medication?"
Empathy and genuine positive regard build trust
Giving personal advice ("If I were you...") fosters dependence and is not therapeutic
Clarifying and seeking validation prevents misunderstanding
Changing the subject when a topic is uncomfortable helps the patient
A private, unhurried setting and cultural awareness improve communication
Closed-ended questions are best for exploring a patient's feelings

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to a tearful, newly diagnosed patient

opts.text: Sit down, give full attention, and allow attentive silence
Use open-ended questions to explore the patient's feelings
Acknowledge the emotion with empathy
Reflect and clarify to validate understanding
Provide accurate information and explore support resources
"Tell me more about what you're feeling."
"Everything happens for a reason."
Sitting quietly with the patient in silence
"Why didn't you call the doctor sooner?"
"You sound frightened about the surgery."
"I know exactly how you feel."
Restating to confirm the patient's meaning
Changing the subject to something cheerful

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Patient says, "I just can't deal with this anymore."
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay with the patient and give full, calm attention
Use an open-ended response to explore the statement
Directly and calmly ask whether the patient is thinking of suicide
Acknowledge the feelings with empathy; avoid false reassurance
Notify the provider, ensure safety, and document
Using open-ended questions and active listening
Offering false reassurance like "Don't worry"
Sitting at eye level and allowing silence
Asking judgmental "why" questions
Reflecting and validating the patient's feelings
Giving personal opinions and advice
Directly assessing safety when the patient voices hopelessness
Changing the subject to avoid an emotional topic
